data.title: BANCOLOMBIA S.A. ANNOUNCES FILING OF THE 2024 ANNUAL REPORT ON FORM 20-F
data.description:
BANCOLOMBIA S.A. ANNOUNCES FILING OF THE 2024 ANNUAL REPORT ON FORM 20-F

BANCOLOMBIA S.A. ANNOUNCES FILING OF THE 2024 ANNUAL REPORT ON FORM 20-F

PR Newswire

MEDELLÍN, Colombia, April 3, 2025

MEDELLÍN, Colombia, April 3, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Bancolombia S.A. (NYSE: CIB) announces that it has filed its annual report on Form 20-F for the year ended December 31, 2024 with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission – SEC.

The annual report can be downloaded from the SEC website www.sec.gov and in the following days will also be made available on Bancolombia's website in the Investor Relations section.

If you need a hard copy of our Form 20-F, please contact IR@bancolombia.com.co.

 

 

 

View original content:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/bancolombia-sa-announces-filing-of-the-2024-annual-report-on-form-20-f-302420259.html

SOURCE Bancolombia S.A.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Thursday at 3 PM ET
data.description:
Trump Tariffs Send Dow Industrials to 1400-Point Decline

U.S. markets slid in their steepest decline in more than two years, as investors grappled with the threat that President Trump's new tariff plan will trigger global retaliation and hurt economic growth.. 

 
The Day Trump's Tariffs Shook Wall Street and Corporate America

Markets tumbled and executives scrambled to grasp the scope and size of President Trump's trade barriers. 

 
Trump's Tariffs Aim to Create a New World Economic Order

His announcement stunned markets. It could also push countries-including the U.S.-to the brink of recession. 

 
Canada to Hit U.S. Autos With Retaliatory Tariffs

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said the tariff could raise up to C$8 billion, which would be used to help workers and companies affected by the Trump tariffs. 

 
Tariffs Will Hit All U.S. Imports. Price Hikes for These Items May Surprise You.

A range of products-think cigars, engagement rings and pineapples-could all have higher price tags soon. 

 
WTO Sees Decline in Trade Flows, Threat of Tariff War

Trade flows would fall further if the U.S. were to respond to any retaliation with further tariff increases, said Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, the WTO's director general. 

 
Canada Swings to Goods-Trade Deficit; Surplus With U.S. Narrows

The value of exports in February dropped 5.5% compared with January to C$70.11 billion, including a 3.6% fall in shipments to the U.S. 

 
Shot by Both Sides, the Eurozone Faces Recession Threat

With the Trump administration targeting its exports, Europe may deal with an economic contraction and the direct impact of lower exports will drag growth down and deliver a shock to confidence. 

 
OPEC+ to Make Larger Output Hike in May, Oil Tumbles

Eight OPEC+ countries will increase output by 411,000 barrels a day next month, prompting oil prices to fall further. 

 
Initial U.S. Jobless Claims Fell Last Week

The week through March 29 brought 219,000 initial jobless claims, compared with 225,000 a week earlier and economists expectations of 228,000 claims.

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

April 03, 2025 15:15 ET (19:15 GMT)

Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Here's how long Trump has to negotiate on tariffs - or risk serious damage to stocks and the U.S. economy
data.description:
By Joseph Adinolfi

One economist estimates the White House has less than two months to reach a resolution that would eliminate most of the targeted reciprocal tariffs. If it doesn't, 2025 could start to look like 2022 for investors.

U.S. stocks were headed for their biggest drop since at least September 2022 on Thursday.

And as investors fretted about the risk that Trump's aggressive approach toward tariffs could spark a global trade war, some on Wall Street said they were surprised that the market wasn't taking an even bigger hit.

"It is surprising that equity prices are not down more," said Neil Dutta, chief economist at Renaissance Macro. "Perhaps this means investors believe negotiations are in store to bring down tariff rates later. I am not so sure."

The expectation that the Trump administration will cut deals with trade partners to reduce or eliminate the tariffs announced on Wednesday appeared to be investors' last, best hope for the U.S. economy to avoid a recession - and a bear market in stocks along with it.

According to a model cited by Dutta, investors already appeared to be pricing in a nearly 90% chance of a recession arriving some time over the next 12 months.

Things were looking dire on Thursday, but some apparently were holding out hope for a resolution that would minimize the economic blowback. In any case, investors would likely need further evidence of slowing growth before sending stocks even lower.

"Things can get worse, but it is worth noting that we have already fallen quite a bit. I could see a scenario where stocks hold up and then fall as growth scare becomes a reality," Dutta said.

A two-month clock

Over the next few months, Wall Street will no doubt be keeping a close eye on economic reports, like Friday's monthly labor-market update, for any signs that souring consumer and investor confidence has already started to seep through to the real economy.

In the meantime, the onus is now on the Trump administration to strike a deal with the European Union, China and other trade partners affected by the specific "reciprocal" tariffs.

Opinion: Will tariffs stick? Trump's history of threats suggests maybe not.

Leaders from both the E.U. and China have hinted that their governments would be open to negotiations, and senior Trump administration officials, including Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, have insisted that the administration won't back off.

Many on Wall Street still expect that the eventual effective tariff rate in the U.S. will end up lower than it will be once Trump's "reciprocal" tariffs take effect next week.

But as one Wall Street economist suggested, time is quickly running out before the economic blowback from Trump's tariffs causes economic damage that could be difficult to reverse. Freya Beamish, an economist at TS Lombard, put the window at two months or less.

"The problem is that we only have a couple of months of leeway for tariffs to remain at these punishing rates without tipping the economy into the nasty regime," Beamish said in a report shared with MarketWatch on Thursday. "In short, the Trump administration probably has less than a couple of months to get these negotiations done, if the plan is to keep tariffs on until resolution is reached."

Two months of these tariffs would push inflation above 4% and deliver the kind of stagflationary shock that is anathema to investors.

"Tariffs are a negative supply shock, which generates the worst kind of inflation. Negative supply shocks are particularly deleterious to wealth because the inflation shock is destroying growth - bad for bonds and equities at the same time," Beamish said.

If the Trump team doesn't start cutting deals soon, 2025 could start to look a lot like 2022, when both stocks and bonds were caught up in a punishing selloff, Beamish added - although, so far, Treasurys have rallied.

U.S. stocks were sharply lower on the day in recent trade, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA off by around 1,390 points, or 3.3%. The Nasdaq Composite COMP was down by roughly 910 points, or 5.2%. The S&P 500 SPX had fallen by 230 points, or 4.1%.

-Joseph Adinolfi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-03-25 1505ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Who's freaking out the most over the stock-market dive? It depends on who they voted for.
data.description:
By Charles Passy

The vibe among Trump supporters is short-term pain for long-term gain. But political views often shape how people feel about the market, experts say.

As investors wrestle with what moves to make with their portfolios amid the current economic turbulence, their decisions may have less to do with any relevant financial factors and more with a choice they made back in November.

Namely, who they voted for in the presidential race.

That's the word from financial advisers and industry experts. They say those who sided with the Republicans and President Donald Trump, whose Wednesday tariff announcement sent an already battered stock market reeling further on Thursday, are likely to be able to put any downturn into context and view it as a case of short-term pain that will eventually result in long-term gain. That means they think tariffs and other moves by the current administration will ultimately improve the bottom line of American consumers and companies, and therefore lift the market.

By contrast, financial pros say those who sided with Democratic candidate and former Vice President Kamala Harris are likely to view the decline - the S&P 500 SPX is now down around 11% since its February peak - in starker terms. They see Trump's policies as potentially leading the country into a recession - or even worse - and are thus inclined to pull money out of the market.

Those in the financial world say this correlation between people's politics and how they view the markets isn't new. Just ask Tom Balcom, a financial adviser based in Lighthouse Point, Fla., who has 120 clients.

"Every four years I go through this," he told MarketWatch, referring to the way his clients' presidential pick often shapes how they want to invest.

There's solid academic research that shows this pattern has long been in place. In a 2012 study titled "Political Climate, Optimism, and Investment Decisions," a trio of researchers found that "individuals become more optimistic and perceive the markets to be less risky and more undervalued when their own party is in power."

Alok Kumar, one of the study's authors and a finance professor at the University of Miami's business school, said the result is that investors tend to validate any good news when their party is in charge, and give less weight to any bad news.

Are things any different today? Not really, most advisers and other financial professionals say. Granted, some note that the country has become more polarized, which means people's views of Trump and his economic policies may be more extreme in either direction. They also note that not all Republicans are completely blasé about the current market decline.

At the same time, they say there was often a similar divide during the Biden administration, with Democrats hopeful about the economy and the market and Republicans mostly on the doom-and-gloom side of the equation. The circumstances and particulars change with each administration, but the biases tend to remain.

Which leads Jason Brown, a stock-market tracker who runs a coaching program called Power Trades University, to quote this aphorism: "History doesn't repeat itself, but it often rhymes."

The result, Brown and others say, is that people often let their political emotions get the best of them, sitting on the sidelines - shifting some or all of their portfolio to safer investments or even to cash - when they would be better off staying in the market. Financial professionals often point out that the market was strongly positive during both President Joe Biden's administration, when the S&P 500 increased 55.7%, and President Trump's first term, when the index was up 67.3%.

"Viewing the market through a partisan lens is dangerous," said Eric Schiffer, an investor and the chair of the Patriarch Organization, a Los Angeles-based venture-capital firm. "Your portfolio isn't bleeding red or blue. It's drowning in your own confirmation bias."

Fabio Ruggeri, founder and chief executive of fintech company MenthorQ, said: "The truth is, markets don't vote - at least not in the way many believe."

In short, Ruggeri is a believer in looking past politics.

"If you were long the S&P 500 at any point over the last 40 years, your cumulative performance was largely unaffected by which party occupied the White House," he said. "The dominant drivers were earnings growth, interest rates, inflation cycles and broader structural shifts - not political leadership alone."

Now read: Is it time to panic about your portfolio? Here's what financial advisers are telling clients.

All of which affirms what advisers and other market experts generally say - that you shouldn't panic during a downturn and that it's almost always best to stay the course. They note that history is a good guide: Callie Cox, chief market strategist at Ritholtz Wealth Management, points out that when the S&P 500 has declined by 20% or more after reaching a 52-week high, it has seen a 17.7% return on average one year later.

"C'mon, how could you argue with these numbers?" Cox wrote.

-Charles Passy

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-03-25 1452ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: BKKT Investors Have Opportunity to Lead Bakkt Holdings, Inc. Securities Fraud Lawsuit with the Schall Law Firm
data.description:
BKKT Investors Have Opportunity to Lead Bakkt Holdings, Inc. Securities Fraud Lawsuit with the Schall Law Firm

The Schall Law Firm, a national shareholder rights litigation firm, reminds investors of a class action lawsuit against Bakkt Holdings, Inc. (“Bakkt” or “the Company”) (NYSE: BKKT) for violations of §§10(b) and 20(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 promulgated thereunder by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.

Investors who purchased the Company’s securities between March 25, 2024 and March 17, 2025, inclusive (the “Class Period”), are encouraged to contact the firm before June 2, 2025.

If you are a shareholder who suffered a loss, click here to participate.

We also encourage you to contact Brian Schall of the Schall Law Firm, 2049 Century Park East, Suite 2460, Los Angeles, CA 90067, at 310-301-3335, to discuss your rights free of charge. You can also reach us through the firm's website at www.schallfirm.com, or by email at bschall@schallfirm.com.

The class, in this case, has not yet been certified, and until certification occurs, you are not represented by an attorney. If you choose to take no action, you can remain an absent class member.

According to the Complaint, the Company made false and misleading statements to the market. Bakkt misrepresented the stability of its crypto business and the diversity of its revenue streams. The Company failed to inform investors that its crypto revenues were substantially reliant on its contract with Webull. The Company misled investors about its ability to sustain relationships with key clients. Based on these facts, the Company’s public statements were false and materially misleading. When the market learned the truth about Bakkt, investors suffered damages.

Join the case to recover your losses.

The Schall Law Firm represents investors around the world and specializes in securities class action lawsuits and shareholder rights litigation.

This press release may be considered Attorney Advertising in some jurisdictions under the applicable law and rules of ethics.

The Schall Law Firm
Brian Schall, Esq.,
www.schallfirm.com

data.title: My grandmother is in her 70s and has $400K in stocks. Is now the time to offload them?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I'm not trying to be overly doomsdayish'

April is National Financial Literacy Month. To mark the occasion, MarketWatch will publish a series of "Financial Fitness" articles to help readers improve their fiscal health, and offer advice on how to save, invest and spend their money wisely. Read more here.

Dear Quentin,

My grandmother is in her late 70s and has about $400,000 investments in the stock market. She has a $250,000 house, on which she has a mortgage with a fairly low interest rate. She has retirement savings and a pension and has never had to pull from her investments.

I'm not trying to harp too much on the current economic state, but I'm a little worried about her losing her investments and still owing money on her home. I'm sorry in advance if this sounds silly or political, and 100% I'm not trying to be overly doomsdayish.

I just want to be sure she won't end up in a situation with nothing, and I am trying to look out for her. She skimps on a lot of things and doesn't have a huge amount of savings in her bank - I'd say she has $5,000. She's had her ups and downs, but overall she is in good health.

At this point, would it make more sense for her to have her home paid off?

The Grandkid

Related: I invested $100,000 in the S&P 500 in February and lost $10,000. How long will it take to recover?

Dear Grandkid,

No wolf, imaginary or otherwise, is scratching at your grandmother's door. Yes, it would make sense.

I received the exact opposite question from a reader recently, wondering whether it was too late for his mother, who is 63, to invest in the stock market. As I told them, a diversified portfolio includes cash, stocks, bonds and non-U.S. stocks. Currently, it looks like 61% of your grandmother's wealth is in stocks, 38% is in real estate and less than 1% is in cash. Ideally, it would be nice if she had more money in an emergency fund. Mortgage payments are not seen as bad debt, but it could also be good to lift that weight from her shoulders. I'd prefer to see 30% in stocks and 30% in cash.

That said, she has amassed a $400,000 portfolio over the last 50 years because she has played the long game. Don't buckle on the last lap. The good news is that your grandmother's investments, as they currently stand, appear to far outweigh the remainder on her mortgage - as long as she did not take out a reverse mortgage in recent years. I'm assuming that the $400,000 in stocks and $5,000 in a savings account accounts for 100% of her net worth (that, and her $250,000 home). That along with her retirement income puts her in pretty good shape.

It appears that your grandmother can continue to make her mortgage payments and live comfortably.

You can occupy a middle ground while you help your grandmother plan her investments and organize her estate for the next 10 or 20 years. I assume your concern is related to the geopolitical situation - the wars in Ukraine and Gaza, and U.S. foreign policy that appears to be breaking with the postwar Western alliance - and to the stock-market volatility in recent months, which is the result at least in part of the Trump administration's proposed tariffs (which may be part of a negotiating strategy; expectations around these appear to be changing weekly or even daily).

You don't say how much your grandmother is paying every month toward her mortgage, whether she has an interest rate below, say, 5%, or how much of her monthly Social Security and pension checks go toward her mortgage. But if she does become sick later in life - and she's still young, if she's in good health by today's standards - and she does not have long-term-care insurance, she would have to spend down all her assets before applying for Medicaid. Medicaid does not have a five-year look-back or "clawback" rule for paying off a mortgage.

Related: 'Is it finally time to freak out?' I'm in my 50s and worried about the $650K in my 401(k).

Martin Schamis, a certified financial planner with Janney Montgomery Scott in Philadelphia, commends your grandmother's prudence and sensible long-term investing, but he has another tax reminder: "Investment assets held in nonqualified accounts will receive a step-up in basis at the death of the owner. While you mention your grandmother is thankfully in good health, this should be considered along with the actual makeup of the investments, the aforementioned embedded gain and your grandmother's need to access the funds."

Given that your grandmother has not needed to pull from her investments thanks to having a healthy pension and retirement funds, Schamis adds, it also makes it more likely that she can continue to make her mortgage payments and live comfortably. "Not having to withdraw from the portfolio in the short term to cover everyday expenses means that she should be less sensitive to the intermittent moves in her investments," he says. "You don't mention the cost basis of her investments, but it sounds as if she's been a long-term investor."

Your grandmother has amassed a $400,000 portfolio over the last 50 years. Don't buckle on the last lap.

Your grandmother could live well into her 90s, so don't make any big, drastic decisions that you may regret later or that could result in a massive capital-gains tax bill. For every investor who is worried about the recent stock-market tumble, there will be another bullish investor who is cheering on changes President Donald Trump is making to U.S. foreign and domestic policy and looking at an uptick in the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA, S&P 500 SPX and Nasdaq COMP and saying, "I told you so!" That investor may have great confidence in the years ahead.

"As many taxpayers sit on long-term positions with unrealized gains - think Nvidia (NVDA) - selling can trigger multiple levels of federal and state taxes, depending on one's situation," says Michael Brennan, a tax adviser with Berkowitz Pollack Brant Advisors and CPAs in New York City. "Additionally, the opportunity cost of a poorly timed and misjudged portfolio drawdown could severely impact long-term financial performance. The age-old saying 'You can't time the market' remains relevant more than ever in today's market conditions."

Related: Will Trump's 'liberation day' tariffs hurt my retirement? I plan to retire in 3 years.

Certain advisable strategies can help mitigate the tax effect of drawing down on positions, Brennan says. "Selling positions within an IRA are generally tax free," he notes. "Starting with positions that have little to no gain or loss is advantageous for taxpayers who want to move more into cash. Harvesting losses from other sources against gains should be analyzed and timed properly to ensure tax efficiency. For taxpayers who are charitably inclined, donating highly appreciated long-term positions can provide a tax deduction without incurring a taxable gain."

Some encouraging data points for bears to chew over: ??The U.S. currently has near full employment. Unemployment was 4.1% in February, up just a tick from 4% in January. Gross domestic product growth in the second quarter outperformed many economists' more cautious predictions. The labor-participation rate hovers around 80%. The annual inflation rate hit 2.8% in February, down from a recent peak of 9.1% in June 2022. Wage growth is still solid, even if consumer spending is showing signs of slowing down.

There will always be an investor who predicts good times, and another who predicts a doomsday scenario.

And some cautious bites for bulls: The federal deficit is now more than 6% of GDP, double what it was a decade ago. The Federal Reserve appears reluctant to make any significant interest-rate cuts anytime soon. The gap between the rich and everyone else has widened, along with their spending habits: The 10% of U.S. households that earn $250,000 or more annually now account for nearly 50% of consumer spending. Consumer confidence in March hit a four-year low. It suggests a worrying dual economy where the other 90% are struggling to make ends meet.

There will always be an investor who predicts good times, and another who predicts doomsday. Economist Harry Dent told Fox News last year: "It is going to be the biggest crash of our lifetime, most of it happening in 2024." Last month, he again predicted a "2008-level market crash." Ditto those who predict great things ahead. Oppenheimer, known as the biggest bull on Wall Street, started the year by predicting the S&P would rise 17%. If they keep predicting good or bad things ahead, who knows - they both may eventually be right.

You and your grandmother don't need to be either bulls or bears. You just need to safeguard her financial stability.

Related: 'I'm absolutely terrible at managing money': I'm 35 with zero savings and $8,000 debt. How do I turn my life around?

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'I'm deeply disturbed': My portfolio lost 20%. With Trump's trade war, do I sell my stocks and buy gold?

My wife and I found two stocks with a 20% return. We have $75,000. Should we invest it in the S&P 500 or individual stocks?

'I'm deeply disturbed': My portfolio lost 20%. With Trump's trade war, do I sell my stocks and buy gold?

Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

04-03-25 1438ET

Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: RH stock is down nearly 40% - and 'fully at the mercy' of tariffs, analyst says
data.description:
By Bill Peters

Stifel analysts said Trump's new tariffs 'quickly commandeered sentiment' over the high-end furniture chain

As President Donald Trump's new tariffs hammered retail and clothing stocks on Thursday, upscale furniture chain RH was among the biggest losers - plunging toward its biggest percentage drop ever as analysts raised concerns about steeper costs and difficulties in finding new ways to secure some of the materials it uses to make products.

The 40% slide came after RH's (RH) earnings release after hours on Wednesday landed roughly at the same time that Trump announced steeper tariffs globally - and particularly on nations like China and Vietnam, where RH makes a significant portion of its furniture.

Stifel analysts, in a note on Wednesday, said the tariffs "quickly commandeered sentiment" as RH's shares began falling after hours that afternoon.

They added that if tariffs became "the new normal, we believe the negative after-hours reaction implies RH is fully at the mercy" of the challenges related to those duties.

"While we continue to see opportunity within the company's new product assortment, the uncertain consumer reaction to tariffs may put additional pressure on consumers to defer big-ticket discretionary purchases," KeyBanc analyst Brad Thomas said in a note on Wednesday.

RH, during its earnings call on Wednesday, said that before Trump's tariff announcement, it had already stocked up on $200 million to $300 million worth of excess furniture and decor that its competitors didn't have. Those rivals, Chief Executive Gary Friedman said, might have to order additional furniture at a much higher cost.

"Inventory is our friend," he noted.

How long it might be the company's friend was up for debate. Wedbush analysts on Thursday estimated that RH's current surplus would likely only give it less than one quarter of sales "cushion" from higher tariff-related costs.

Friedman, during the call, tried to highlight the potential longer-term positives of Trump's trade escalations. RH has said it has been trying to rely less on China and Mexico, another target of Trump's tariffs, for production. In December, the company said it expected to make a full exit from China by the end of its second quarter.

However, the Wedbush analysts noted that management didn't reiterate that timeline on Wednesday. And according to RH's annual report filed on Wednesday, 72% of its products came from Asia in its fiscal 2024 when measured by dollar volume of purchases. Thirty-five percent came from Vietnam, 23% came from China, and the remaining portion largely came from Indonesia and India, that report said.

The Wedbush analysts said the tariffs placed on most of the nations where RH sources products could translate to a 38% jump in production-related costs for its merchandise, if it didn't shift manufacturing elsewhere. But even with changes the analysts assumed could take hold this year, those costs would still be up nearly 30%, they estimated.

The analysts also noted that RH on Wednesday called out the difficulties in pivoting away from nations like Indonesia, where it gets a lot of its teak used to make outdoor furniture. Friedman, during the call, said that Indonesia was "the capital of premium teak."

"With that said, we believe RH is better positioned than most discretionary retailers given its scale and higher-net-worth customer base," the analysts said.

Trump has said that the tariffs will help the U.S. negotiate more favorable deals with other nations and replenish the nation's manufacturing base by deterring companies from making products abroad.

But many economists have worried those tariffs will raise costs for consumers. Markets retreated on Thursday amid fears of a recession and disruptions to the global trade order. Within the housing industry, which often plays a big role in the furniture industry's fortunes, analysts expect higher home-building and remodeling costs, but potentially lower mortgage rates.

Retail groups, either way, didn't take Trump's announcement well. The National Retail Federation, in a statement on Wednesday, said the tariffs would further squeeze consumers who have been wrestling with price increases for the past few years.

"More tariffs equal more anxiety and uncertainty for American businesses and consumers," David French, the industry group's executive vice president of government relations, said in a statement. "While leaders in Washington may not care about higher prices, hardworking American families do."

The Home Furnishings Association, an industry advocacy group, said in a statement that "unfortunately, this round of tariff increases will negatively impact all stakeholders in the home-furnishings ecosystem.

"Even domestic manufacturers, who may not directly import finished goods, rely on imported components such as hardware, upholstery and cut-and-sew kits," it added.

The group said it was organizing a gathering in Washington next month, where retailers could meet with their state representatives to express their concerns.

-Bill Peters

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-03-25 1439ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: PERFECT Sports® Teams Up with Marvel Studios' "Thunderbolts*", Only In Theaters May 2
data.description:
PERFECT Sports® Teams Up with Marvel Studios' "Thunderbolts*", Only In Theaters May 2

PERFECT Sports® Teams Up with Marvel Studios' "Thunderbolts*", Only In Theaters May 2

PR Newswire

TORONTO, April 3, 2025

A Bold Collaboration Celebrating Strength, Redemption, and Relentless Determination

TORONTO, April 3, 2025 /PRNewswire/ - PERFECT Sports, makers of the DIESEL® New Zealand Protein, is proud to announce an exclusive collaboration with Marvel Studios' "Thunderbolts*", in theaters May 2. 

This marks another major milestone for PERFECT Sports after the recent smash success of their collab with Marvel Studios' "Captain America: Brave New World" in Canada. Like the team of anti-heroes in "Thunderbolts*", PERFECT Sports is committed to helping individuals aspire to greatness. 

The promotion, running from April 1 to June 30, 2025, celebrates the release of "Thunderbolts*" through exciting activations and limited-edition products for customers across Canada and the United States. As part of the collaboration, PERFECT Sports is proud to sponsor the world premiere, aligning the brand with one of the most anticipated films of the year.

"At PERFECT Sports, we believe that greatness comes from resilience, purpose, and pushing past your limits," said Bruce Colero, Owner of PERFECT Sports. "Teaming up with Marvel Studios' 'Thunderbolts*'—a film about redemption and rising to face impossible odds—is a natural fit. It's not just an exciting moment for our brand; it's an expression of our core belief: to BE GREAT.™"

PERFECT Sports speaks for everyone who wants to be the best version of themselves, for those who want to RISE UP and BE GREAT.™ The wait is over! Limited-edition "Thunderbolts*"-inspired products from PERFECT Sports are coming to a retailer near you. Find a location in your area at www.perfectsports.com and follow @perfectsports on social media.

See Marvel Studios' "Thunderbolts*" when it hits theaters on May 2.

About PERFECT Sports

Founded on the principles of innovation, quality, and integrity, PERFECT Sports is Canada's #1 selling whey protein brand that delivers premium, clean and effective nutrition. From elite-level pro athletes to people who want the most premium nutrition, PERFECT Sports® delivers WADA-tested, truly clean, and highly effective nutrition to power your journey. Whether it's DIESEL New Zealand Whey Protein, DIESEL New Zealand Protein Bars, ULTRA FUEL Whey Protein, or Essential Creatine, PERFECT Sports wants to help you achieve more, to grow past your limits, to BE GREAT.™

PERFECT Sports, SVP Marketing
Jason Bell
jay@perfectsports.com

About Marvel Studios' Thunderbolts*

In "Thunderbolts*" Marvel Studios assembles an unconventional team of antiheroes— Yelena Belova, Bucky Barnes, Red Guardian, Ghost, Taskmaster and John Walker. After finding themselves ensnared in a death trap set by Valentina Allegra de Fontaine, these disillusioned castoffs must embark on a dangerous mission that will force them to confront the darkest corners of their pasts. Will this dysfunctional group tear themselves apart, or find redemption and unite as something much more before it's too late?

The film stars Florence Pugh, Sebastian Stan, Wyatt Russell, Olga Kurylenko, Lewis Pullman, Geraldine Viswanathan, Chris Bauer, Wendell Edward Pierce, with David Harbour, with Hannah John-Kamen, and Julia Louis-Dreyfus.

Jake Schreier directs "Thunderbolts*" and Kevin Feige is the producer. Louis D'Esposito, Brian Chapek and Jason Tamez serve as executive producers. The screenplay was written by Joanna Calo and Eric Pearson and Lee Sung Jin. 

Marvel Studios' "Thunderbolts*" releases only in theaters May 2.

data.title: Vagaro Vantage Report Explores Why Employment in Barber & Beauty Industries Projected to Grow Faster Than Most Industries
data.description:
Vagaro Vantage Report Explores Why Employment in Barber & Beauty Industries Projected to Grow Faster Than Most Industries

While beauty and barber industries are experiencing continued growth; Vagaro, the leading software for businesses in beauty, wellness, and fitness, recently released their Vantage Report which explores this being driven by evolving trends, digital transformation, and shifting consumer behaviors.

According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, employment for barbers, hairstylists, and cosmetologists is projected to grow 7% through 2033—faster than the average for most occupations.

This translates to an estimated 45,300 new jobs over the next decade, with around 89,100 job openings annually due to retirements and career transitions.

The growth is fueled by several key factors. Despite economic fluctuations, people continue to prioritize looking good and feeling their best, a phenomenon known as the “lipstick effect.” As the U.S. population grows, so does the demand for beauty services.

According to the 2025 Vagaro Vantage Report, services like lash and brow treatments have seen a surge, accounting for 71% of searches on the Vagaro Marketplace. Services requiring regular maintenance, such as lash fills and brow touch-ups, provide a steady stream of business for beauty professionals.

Social media platforms have amplified the spread of beauty trends, sparking viral interest in styles such as "expensive brunette" hair colors, nail art, and scalp facials. Additionally, the beauty industry has become more inclusive, with many businesses increasingly catering to neurodivergent clients and the LGBTQIA+ community, creating safe and welcoming spaces.

The barbering industry specifically is evolving beyond traditional haircuts to include services such as skincare, scalp treatments, men’s grooming, and specialized color services.

This expansion creates more job opportunities and increases earning potential for skilled professionals. Celebrity barber, Vagaro Icon Coach, and Founder & CEO of Popular Nobody John Mosley, highlights this shift: “There is a rise in different hair services being sought after, beyond just simple haircuts.”

As the industry grows, so does the competition. Beauty professionals can capitalize on this growth by offering apprenticeships and mentorships, helping to train the next generation of talent.

Fresh talent is also an opportunity for salons to train professionals without pre-existing habits, ensuring high-quality service. Continuing education, such as courses in cutting, coloring, or business management, keeps professionals competitive and current with industry trends.

Technology is playing a vital role in the industry’s growth. Digital tools like AI, automation, and booking systems help salons scale and attract clients. According to the Vagaro Vantage Report, two-thirds of beauty professionals say AI streamlines their operations, while 75% prioritize scalability.

The barber & beauty industry has proven its resilience and growth, offering exciting opportunities for ambitious professionals. Now is the time to embrace new trends, refine skills, and grow in this thriving industry.

About Vagaro

Vagaro is the leading salon, spa, and fitness software, serving hundreds of thousands of professionals worldwide. Vagaro simplifies business management, credit card and payment processing, and makes it easy for businesses to grow their clientele on a modern consumer marketplace. Vagaro's a-la-carte options and affordable pricing provide a unique level of scalability, making it suitable for businesses of all sizes, from the solopreneur to enterprise franchises. Simple, innovative, and reliable, Vagaro empowers beauty and wellness professionals to excel in a digital age. Visit Vagaro Pro to learn more.

press@vagaro.com
